title: Fitting a door handle and latch set
subtitle: Mortise the door edge, bore the handle holes, and fit the strike plate
excerpt: How to fit a tubular mortise latch and lever handle set on an internal door: marking the backset, chiselling the mortise and face-plate recess, boring the spindle and handle holes, and fitting the keep plate on the door frame.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A door latch that fits into a rectangular slot (mortise) cut into the door edge. The tubular version requires a 22 to 25 mm (7/8 to 1 in) diameter hole through the door face and a mortise in the door edge for the latch body.

The distance from the front face of the door edge to the centre of the latch spindle hole. Standard backsets are 44 mm and 57 mm (1 3/4 in and 2 1/4 in); the backset must match the distance from the door edge to the centre of the handle hole.

content.text: fits into the door edge; the

must match the distance from the door edge to the spindle centre. Check the backset on the handle packaging before buying; most sets are 44 mm or 57 mm. Confirm the door thickness is at least 35 mm (1 3/8 in) for a standard tubular latch.

Standard door handle height is 1000 mm (39 in) from the floor to the spindle centre, though this can vary by 50 mm (2 in) for comfort. Mark the spindle centre on both the door face and the door edge using a combination square to transfer the mark accurately. Hold the latch body against the door edge and mark the top and bottom of the mortise slot.
Step 2, chisel the mortise in the door edge

Drill a series of holes across the mortise area using a bit matching the latch body width (typically 19 or 22 mm). Remove the waste between holes with a chisel and mallet, working from the centre outward. Check the latch body drops in flush with the door edge face. Do not widen the mortise with horizontal leverage; pare vertically and check fit frequently.

Fit the latch body into the mortise and mark the face plate outline with a utility knife. Remove the latch and pare a shallow recess to the face plate depth (typically 2 mm) so the plate sits flush with the door edge. A proud face plate causes the door to bind against the frame. Mark and drill the spindle hole (25 mm forstner bit for most sets) and the rose-plate screw holes through the face.
Step 4, bore the handle holes and fit

Bore the handle hole with a 22 to 25 mm (7/8 to 1 in) spade or forstner bit from one face to halfway through the door, then complete from the opposite face to prevent breakout. Fit the latch body, thread the spindle through, and fit both handles. The handle rose plates should sit tight to the door face; adjust the spindle length if the handles sit proud of the roses.
Step 5, fit the keep plate on the frame

Close the door and mark the latch bolt position on the frame by pressing a piece of chalk against the bolt face and closing gently. Mark the keep plate outline and chisel a recess for both the box and the plate face. The latch bolt must enter the keep box cleanly on the first close. Adjust by paring the recess rather than by moving the keep: a keep moved outward weakens the frame.

attrs.symptom: Latch bolt does not reach the keep when the door is closed
attrs.cause: The keep plate box is recessed too deep, or the door has dropped on its hinges since fitting.
attrs.fix: Pack the keep box with a piece of cardboard or thin timber to bring it forward, or add a packing piece under the hinges to raise the door.

attrs.cause: The face plate is sitting proud of the door edge and binding, or the spindle is too long and is putting pressure on the handle roses.
attrs.fix: Remove the face plate and deepen the recess by 1 mm. Check the spindle length and trim if the handles sit out from the door face.
